Okay, so we're going to talk about the creative brief. And it's something that you should also give to any designers that you work with. You know, anybody that's involved in the project. They're marketing people. It's so useful. The first thing, of course, you want is the background. And there's three different kinds of background, right? There's of course the background of the product. What is it? What does it do? How does it do it? How is it made? What are the components of it? As much as you can get. Writer can't have too much background because you never know where a writer or where you are going to find that little nugget that's going to make the copy sing. That's going to make the copy so interesting. That's going to get the attention of the reader. That's going to convince the reader. So background about the product. Background about the market. How big is it? How many people? Where do they live? What types of things do they do? The stuff we talked about and then about overall. But that's also the individual. Who is this one person that we're writing to? What are his loves, hates, desires, secret fears? All that kind of thing. And then of course a good writer will want to know about the competition. What are other newsletters that go to investors? What are other newsletters that go to purchasing managers, human resource people? How do they sell themselves? What do they do? What have they done that's worked? What have they done that hasn't worked? So that's direct. But there's also indirect competition, which is a newsletter isn't necessarily the only way that human resources get, human resource managers get information. So what about seminars? How do they market themselves to human resource managers? What can we learn from them? What are other ways investors get? In a way, television is competition. Magazines are competition. All these people are competition for your clients' dollars, for your prospects' dollars. And what they're doing, we can learn from that as writers. We go, oh, that's a clever way to get their attention. That's a clever way to sell something. So you want to give all sorts of competitive information to the writer. Of course you want to know what the objectives are. What are we really trying to accomplish? We're trying to generate more traffic. Are we trying to get... We got enough traffic. Are we trying to get more conversions? Are we trying to get people to think more positively about it? Is the newsletter very expensive? So our objective is to convince people that it's not really that expensive, that it's worth paying for. And objectives are just constantly changing. It's always like, oh, now we need more traffic. Now we need more this. Now we need more front end. Now we need more back end. What's worked and more importantly, what's not worked. What's worked tells us a lot about the prospect. Tells us a lot about what appeals to them. Tells us a lot about what their hot buttons are. Tells us a lot about what kind of language to use. What hasn't worked also tells us a lot. It tells us what language doesn't work. It tells us maybe what offends them, what turns them off, what they're just not interested in. It tells us what to not do. Like, oh, I've got an idea. Why don't we say that this will not only help you get more money, it'll help you live longer, whatever it is. And then you see, oh, look, they did a promotion like that. It didn't work. So it helps us be more exact in what we know will work because we've already seen what doesn't work. We won't do that again. We won't go down that road again. So important because clients, I really have to argue with clients to send me things that haven't worked because they're like, oh, no, that didn't work. You wouldn't be interested in that. You know, they're kind of embarrassed by it. But no, you know, bring it out. We need to study it. Copy points. What are the copy points about this product? What are the benefits? What are the things that we say about it that we have found work? What are the things that our people say over the telephone that consumers react positively to? What are the types of language that we use when talking about this product? What are the bullet points about this product? The main features, the main benefits. As much as you can give a writer, as much as you can give yourself down on paper, then it's all right there for you. I want to start writing. I look, oh, look, there's some copy points. Oh, I see. I can write that. I can put that in different words. Makes it so easy when you've got that down on paper. And then resources. Richard Armstrong did that package tsunami for Weiss. And, you know, Clayton was saying he was amazed. He said, Richard wouldn't even start the package until he had read six books about options trading or whatever this particular package was going to be about. Six books, you know? And that's how it is with writers. They want the resources because that's where they find nuggets. That's where they find ideas. That's where they become the expert. And there's copy sounds like they're the expert about this topic. So of course, you've got books. You've got articles. You've got a million links, right? Here's a website for our competitors. Here's a website for where you get information about this. Here's a website that human resource managers go to all the time. Here's a discussion forum. You should go here and eavesdrop on them. People. Really important. The best copy comes out of the mouths of people. I don't know if I told this story here in the other group about Gene Schwartz, and he had to do the first promotion ever for boardroom. And it was like how you could... Boardroom was little summaries of articles from magazines, little summaries of books, little nuggets of information. And so he went and talked to Marty Edelson, and Marty talked for two hours. And Gene Schwartz said, all I did was listen. He said I could have gone out of his office and written down the ad just from all the things he said, but I had a wait a week so he would think I was working really hard on it. So Marty Edelson said, this is like getting 50 books in 15 minutes or something like that. Gene Schwartz wrote this book, 15 books, 15 minutes. Oh, here's your headline, 15 books in 15 minutes. And all the other copy, because Marty was so knowledgeable about it. He was so enthusiastic about it. It was a great copy. Gene Schwartz liked to say, good copy is just arranging. All I do is arrange things. I take this information, put it here. It's stronger here. So give people to talk to. The people in telemarketing, who are talking day in and day out declines. People in the mail room. What's the mail that comes back? What kind of mail do we get from the people that we talk to? If you've got salespeople, if you've got someone on your staff that's really knowledgeable about a certain thing, let the copywriter talk to the editor. A lot of times, there's like this wall between the copywriter and the editor. It's got to go through the product person. And when the copywriter talks to the editor, very magical things happen. All of a sudden, the copywriter understands things. They have a feeling for who this person is. They start to have a voice. Oh, I hear the editor's voice. Now I write in his voice. A lot of times, it helps because the editor then also understands better what it is that copywriters do. Because a lot of times, of course, you've probably had this experience. An editor will read the copy and go, why do you have to write such hype copy? Why do you have to say these things? But the copywriter can kind of explain to him, look, this is how you've got to get people interested. You've got to set the page on fire. If we don't get people interested, no one's going to read your great words. The editor goes, I guess he's right. Got a point there. So when you bring copywriters and editors together, it's so important. In resources, give them the names of people that they can contact. Let them talk to customers. Editor, customers, telemarketers. And then, of course, resources also includes what are the resources they can use to put this letter together. Here's a list of testimonials. Here's a list of photographs. Go to this place and you can find all the photographs that we've taken of the product of the editor. And this will give you an idea for how to use them in your copy. And as I say, even the copywriter, it's very important to him that the picture of the editor be the right picture. And look, here's a picture of the editor mountain climbing. Oh, I didn't know he did mountain climbing. I want to write a little bit about it in there and then show this picture. And here's a little caption to go under it. Sometimes I'll look at a bunch of winners and I'll look at a bunch of losers and I'll look at the winners. I'll say, you know, all of these winners talk about the pain of the prospect or something and look at this, all of the losers don't. Or all of the winners have a big picture of the doctor looking, right? Didn't we, for Sears, wasn't there something that, like, all of the winners had a certain look of Sears, you know, out in front or something? I can't even remember what it was. But you just start to get a sense after a while. This is what makes for winners for this product. Yeah, this is kind of what makes for losers. I can see that. It's the headlines are not concrete. The headlines all don't promise a benefit. All these winners have big benefit promises.
